ks1.objective: Run between four castles without being caught by dragons. Learn the basic chase-and-evade mechanic before footballs are added.
successCriteria: Runs in and out rather than freezing in fear
Plays both knight and dragon when swapped

lks2.objective: Move with a football between castles, keeping it close while dragons attempt to tag the ball.
successCriteria: Keeps the ball within one step of their feet
Spots where the dragon is before committing
Changes direction when a dragon closes in

uks2.objective: Dribble through dragon-occupied castles using changes of pace and body shape. Read the dragon before committing to a route.
successCriteria: Uses a change of pace to escape a dragon
Disguises direction with body shape or a feint
Picks the riskier or safer route deliberately

space.description: A large rectangular play area with four 'castles' marked roughly at each corner of the inner zone. Each castle is a square shape made from triangular cone formations. Plenty of open space between castles for running.

value.name: Bravery
value.text: Bravery through stepping into risk. Running past a dragon into the next castle takes courage — especially for the quieter children. Praise the choice to keep moving, not just the success of getting through. Make it visible when someone tries a risky route, even if they get tagged.

steps: Set up four castle areas (squares) in the corners of the play space — mark each with triangular cone formations.
Begin without the footballs. Children are knights — their job is to travel in and out of the different castles.
Award different points depending on which side of the castle the knight leaves from — keeps movement varied.
Once they understand the layout, introduce dragons (taggers) inside the castles whose job is to tag knights running through.
When a knight is tagged, they swap roles with the dragon or perform a quick task before re-entering.
progressions: Change the dragons regularly so every child has a go at both roles.
Introduce the footballs — knights now carry their ball from castle to castle. Dragons try to touch the football.
Allow knights to bounce the ball between castles, or throw and catch it on the move.
Move the footballs to the floor — knights must dribble through the castles.
Allow the dragons to leave their castle area to tackle the knights in the open space.
questions: How did it feel to run through a castle when you could see a dragon waiting?
What did you do with your body to make the dragon misread where you were going?
Which castle was easiest? Which was hardest? Why?

step.space: Smaller castles = harder for knights to navigate; larger castles = harder for dragons to defend.
step.task: Set individual challenges for top performers when they move between castles (weaker foot only, specific surface of the foot).
step.equipment: Add gates inside each castle the knights must dribble through; or vary cone colours so different exits earn different points.
step.people: Pair shy children with confident teammates. Let the bravest go first to model courage.

base.showmanship: Use storytelling to capture the children's imagination. Start with 'Once upon a time, in a kingdom of brave knights...'. Name each castle (red castle, dragon's castle, gold castle). Praise loudly when a quieter child takes the leap into a defended castle — 'That's a brave knight right there!'
